comment.body:
SPX Game Plan – Wed, Apr 02  
(Liberation Day! Except Trump called his announcement for 4 PM, so we're really in the holding cell awaiting our discharge papers and the conditions of our parole)

—

CONTEXT  
• Yesterday = wide swings + EOD dealer rally  
• GEX still thin & fragmented both ways → dealers short puts & calls  
• Overnight: 5600–5650 | E-mini straddle implies 5550–5650 range  
• Catalysts: ADP private payrolls + 4PM Trump presser  
• Current structure favors chop, large swings, tail reactivity  
• 5600–5620 = high chop risk / neutral zone  
• 5540–5505 = unstable footing  
• 5640–5670 = resistance shelf

—

KEY LEVELS & STRUCTURE  
• Put OI: Far OTM 5505↓, 5505–5550 OTM, 5575–95 NTM, 5615/40 ITM  
• Call OI: 5550/60 call debit spread = support/pivot, 5600 NTM, 5660+ OTM  
• Strong GEX Zones: 5500/05, 5550/60, 5640, 5695  
• 20D Strikes: 5540P / 5655C (monitor those deltas intraday — they’ll shift with changes in SPX spot and IV)  
• Tue Range: 5555–5645 | WTD: 5490–5650  
• 5600 area = trap-prone; needs volume or vol crush for escape

—

SPX ROAD MAP – 0DTE  
Chop Zone: 5560–5620  
• 5600 = Pivot BMO  
• IF sticky → path to 5615 or 5580  
• < 5580/85 → 5560 magnet; may stall ~5570 before continuation  
• > 5600 → grind to 5615/20

Above:  
• > 5620 → test 5630/35 → 5640  
• > 5640 → 5650 round number  
• > 5650 → stall near 5660, stretch target = 5676/5700

Below:  
• = 5560 → reversal or continuation  
• < 5560 → 5550  
• < 5550 → 5540 (gamma flip = unstable)  
• < 5540 → 5520/25 → 5505/00 → reversal zone toward 5550

—

VOLATILITY & POSITIONING INSIGHTS  
VIX Structure (6:45 AM):  
VIX1D (20) < VIX9D (24.5) > $VIX (22.9) > VIX3M (21.8) > VX (21.5)  
VVIX = 97.5  
• Short-term backwardation → elevated front vol, mixed message beyond  
• 9D pumped for tariffs/NFP/political risk  
• Spot vol in the middle → market unsure  
• VVIX elevated = wings sticky, not decompressing cleanly  
• VOLI + TDEX softening, but far OTM tails still bid  
• Expect vol to stay bid through early session → decompress possible post-4PM

—

TRADE STRUCTURING & EXECUTION  
• Scalp bias around 5600 pivot  
• Charm fuel potential >5550/60  
• Fade edges unless vol compresses  
• Avoid chasing >5650 unless clean break + vol drops  
• Reversal setups near 5540 or 5670 edges

—

POST-TRADE REVIEW & ADJUSTMENTS  
• Monitor reactions at 5600 → 5615 or 5580  
• Charm + EOD rally >5615 possible if vol drops  
• Watch for ADP fake + fade → macro flip risk

—

ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS  
• GEX profile = thin, wingy, reactive  
• 5600–5620 = chop trap zone  
• 5640–5670 = supply shelf  
• 5540–5505 = prone to slippery downside extensions  
• Vol compressible after 4PM if presser underwhelms

—

Wider Levels / MTF Notes  
• Weekly range: 5490–5650  
• Above: 5660 → 5676 → 5700  
• Below: 5525 → 5505 → 5490  
• Gamma flip zone = 5540  
• 20D inflection still key: 5540P / 5655C  
• Monitor those 20D deltas intraday — they’ll shift with changes in SPX spot and IV.

comment.body: So upside intraday mean reversion is starting to fade, but we still need lower RV to realize a drop. If we remain below 5722 this is just common mean reversion trading that doesn’t mean we are on the way higher. However, if we are above 5722 and volatility (vix and RV?) is down, then we might see higher prices continuing a more convincing “bull trap” rally? Goal is for 20RV on the Trading View indicator to reach about 16% for lower volatility requirements to be realized. If we get that above 5722 and it is lower than that, just icing on the cake to further confirm eventual pending downside?

comment.body: Its sorta illogical. Musk is cutting government spending, which as long as the savings are used to reduce the deficit is a cut of 1% of GDP so far. Trump is raising Tariffs, folk mistakenly see this as inflationary. It is not. Imagine you live in an economy with 3 consumers with £120 and one supplier of food (banana's). The banana supplier has 6 bananas. what is the price of banana's...£20each.  Now the ffod supplier trys to incraese prices to £30....but there is still only £120 in the economy....he either lets the banana's rot or he has to reduce his price...... Now if you introduce a bank that has £120 to loan what is the price of bananas then.....£40. Its the increase in money supply that increases prices not the price of the product. If the price increases with same amount of money in supply it either rots or prices fall back to normal

comment.body:
Alma did say algos are in mean reversion mode, even though it is becoming less and less pronounced. Seems though that we should go higher and it seems highly probably given sentiment… although what’s weird is that there is not a lot of hedging to the downside. So as Alma has alluded to, the market is structurally set up to continue down. Moreover, even though she does say vol should come down, if you look at 22’, VIX never pierced below $18.50 outside of January. And it raged up to $34ish for its highs.

We are coming off a $16.50 low, and during 22’, we’ve seen those wild spikes where it didn’t need to consolidate at these levels. So we are really in a coin toss environment here. Although it still does seem like further upside is likely.

comment.body:
Based on my search, here's what it would take for Congress to override presidential tariffs:

For Congress to override presidential tariffs, they would need to pass specific legislation through both chambers that would either require a joint congressional resolution which would need to pass both chambers of Congress (currently Republican-controlled) and either get the president's signature or override his veto with a two-thirds majority in both chambers.

Congress could pass new laws like the recently introduced "Stopping Tariffs on Allies and Bolstering Legislative Exercise of (STABLE) Trade Policy Act" which would require congressional approval before a president could impose new tariffs on U.S. allies and free trade agreement partners.

The challenge is significant because over decades, Congress has gradually delegated extensive tariff authority to the executive branch. The president currently has multiple legal pathways to impose tariffs including Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 (previously used for China tariffs), Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (used for steel and aluminum tariffs), the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), and Section 122 Balance of Payments Authority.

In the current political environment, it would be extremely difficult to pass such legislation given the need for bipartisan cooperation and potentially overcoming a presidential veto.

comment.body:
Based on my search, here's what it would take for Congress to override presidential tariffs:

For Congress to override presidential tariffs, they would need to pass specific legislation through both chambers that would either require a joint congressional resolution which would need to pass both chambers of Congress (currently Republican-controlled) and either get the president's signature or override his veto with a two-thirds majority in both chambers.

Congress could pass new laws like the recently introduced "Stopping Tariffs on Allies and Bolstering Legislative Exercise of (STABLE) Trade Policy Act" which would require congressional approval before a president could impose new tariffs on U.S. allies and free trade agreement partners.

The challenge is significant because over decades, Congress has gradually delegated extensive tariff authority to the executive branch. The president currently has multiple legal pathways to impose tariffs including Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 (previously used for China tariffs), Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (used for steel and aluminum tariffs), the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), and Section 122 Balance of Payments Authority.

In the current political environment, it would be extremely difficult to pass such legislation given the need for bipartisan cooperation and potentially overcoming a presidential veto.

comment.body:
I understand you're asking about tariffs between Korea and the US, specifically about a 50% figure you've encountered. You're correct that the US and Korea have a Free Trade Agreement (KORUS FTA) which eliminates tariffs on most goods traded between the two countries.

The 50% figure you mentioned appears to be derived from dividing exports by imports in a tariff announcement, rather than representing an actual tariff rate. This calculation method is interesting and somewhat unusual in trade discussions.

comment.body:
Based on my search, I can explain whether the 50% figure derived from the exports-to-imports ratio would have real effects on trade or be largely cosmetic:

### The Real vs. Cosmetic Impact

The actual tariff situation between the US and South Korea is very different from what might be suggested by the 50% exports-to-imports ratio. South Korea's Industry Ministry has clarified that "under the Korea-US Free Trade Agreement, both Korea and the US have eliminated tariffs on most goods. As of 2024, Korea's effective tariff rate for goods imported from the US is approximately 0.79 percent."

South Korea's finance minister (acting as president) has stated that "the impact of the reciprocal tariff measures may not be huge on our economy, as tariff rates are low due to the Korea-U.S. Free Trade Agreement." However, he noted that monitoring is necessary since the US indicated it would also assess non-tariff barriers.

The newly announced reciprocal tariffs include a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States, with higher duties for certain countries. Trading partners are expected to respond with countermeasures, potentially leading to higher prices for many goods.

### What This Means in Practice

The 50% figure derived from dividing exports by imports appears to be more of a rhetorical or political calculation rather than an actual tariff rate that would be applied in practice. Under the KORUS FTA, which entered into force in 2012, Korea and the US have already eliminated duties on nearly 80% of bilateral trade in industrial and consumer goods, with most other duties phased out over 10 years and eliminated as of January 1, 2021.

However, despite the low effective tariff rates under the FTA, South Korea is taking the threat seriously. South Korea's acting President has already ordered emergency support measures for industries and businesses that will be impacted by the US's sweeping tariffs and urged the ministry to closely analyze the details and actively negotiate with Washington to minimize the impact.

South Korea has been actively working to secure exemptions from these new tariffs. The South Korean trade minister has already met with the US Trade Representative and asked for the country to be exempted from reciprocal tariffs.

The real economic impact would depend on whether the US actually imposes new tariffs beyond what's already established in the KORUS FTA, particularly focusing on non-tariff barriers. The 50% figure itself appears to be more of a talking point than a policy that would be directly implemented as a tariff rate.

comment.body:
Based on my search, I can explain whether the 50% figure derived from the exports-to-imports ratio would have real effects on trade or be largely cosmetic:

### The Real vs. Cosmetic Impact

The actual tariff situation between the US and South Korea is very different from what might be suggested by the 50% exports-to-imports ratio. South Korea's Industry Ministry has clarified that "under the Korea-US Free Trade Agreement, both Korea and the US have eliminated tariffs on most goods. As of 2024, Korea's effective tariff rate for goods imported from the US is approximately 0.79 percent."

South Korea's finance minister (acting as president) has stated that "the impact of the reciprocal tariff measures may not be huge on our economy, as tariff rates are low due to the Korea-U.S. Free Trade Agreement." However, he noted that monitoring is necessary since the US indicated it would also assess non-tariff barriers.

The newly announced reciprocal tariffs include a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States, with higher duties for certain countries. Trading partners are expected to respond with countermeasures, potentially leading to higher prices for many goods.

### What This Means in Practice

The 50% figure derived from dividing exports by imports appears to be more of a rhetorical or political calculation rather than an actual tariff rate that would be applied in practice. Under the KORUS FTA, which entered into force in 2012, Korea and the US have already eliminated duties on nearly 80% of bilateral trade in industrial and consumer goods, with most other duties phased out over 10 years and eliminated as of January 1, 2021.

However, despite the low effective tariff rates under the FTA, South Korea is taking the threat seriously. South Korea's acting President has already ordered emergency support measures for industries and businesses that will be impacted by the US's sweeping tariffs and urged the ministry to closely analyze the details and actively negotiate with Washington to minimize the impact.

South Korea has been actively working to secure exemptions from these new tariffs. The South Korean trade minister has already met with the US Trade Representative and asked for the country to be exempted from reciprocal tariffs.

The real economic impact would depend on whether the US actually imposes new tariffs beyond what's already established in the KORUS FTA, particularly focusing on non-tariff barriers. The 50% figure itself appears to be more of a talking point than a policy that would be directly implemented as a tariff rate.
